fast.summary: This paper addresses the delayed feedback problem in conversion rate (CVR) prediction for online advertising, where the gap between a user clicking an ad and eventually converting introduces bias in training data. The authors propose using post-click user behaviors (which occur sooner than conversions) as auxiliary signals in a multi-task learning framework, combined with a parameterized scaling technique to improve calibration.
fast.key_idea: Post-click behaviors (e.g., add-to-cart, wishlist) serve as fast-arriving proxy signals that can be used as predictable targets to reduce the bias from delayed conversion labels and improve CVR calibration.
fast.why_it_matters: Miscalibrated CVR predictions directly hurt ad auction bidding strategies and ranking quality, costing advertisers money and reducing platform revenue; better-calibrated models lead to fairer auctions and more efficient budget allocation.
possible_use_cases: Real-time bidding systems where conversion labels arrive hours or days late but click-through behaviors are immediately observable
E-commerce ad platforms needing accurate purchase probability estimates to rank ads by expected value
Subscription or lead-generation campaigns where calibrated conversion probabilities improve cost-per-acquisition targeting

deep.method_breakdown: The method uses post-click user behaviors (e.g., add-to-cart, wishlist, page dwell time) as auxiliary prediction targets in a multi-task learning framework, treating them as intermediate signals that occur faster than actual conversions. An adaptive loss function weighted by task uncertainty (homoscedastic uncertainty, à la Kendall & Gal) balances the contribution of each task without manual tuning. Finally, a parameterized post-hoc scaling layer (akin to temperature scaling or Platt scaling) is applied on top of the multi-task model output to correct residual calibration error.
deep.key_innovation: The core novelty is treating post-click behaviors not merely as auxiliary features or attention signals (as in ESMM/ESM2) but as explicit prediction targets in a multi-task setup, combined with uncertainty-weighted loss balancing and a learnable post-hoc calibration module. Prior delayed feedback work (DFEM, ES-DFM, FSIW) focuses on re-weighting observed labels or imputing missing conversions; this paper instead sidesteps the label-delay problem by exploiting the shorter temporal horizon of behavioral signals to produce a well-calibrated CVR estimate without waiting for ground-truth conversions.
deep.technical_insight: The most critical design choice is using task uncertainty to adaptively weight losses across tasks. Because post-click behavior rates and final conversion rates have very different magnitudes and noise levels, a fixed weighting would dominate training with the easier/noisier task. Uncertainty-based weighting lets the model automatically down-weight tasks it is already confident about, keeping gradient flow balanced and preventing the auxiliary behavior tasks from overwhelming the primary CVR objective — this is what makes the multi-task signal beneficial rather than harmful.
deep.limitations: 1) Post-click behavior signals may themselves be sparse for rare product categories or cold-start items, limiting their utility. 2) The parameterized scaling step requires a held-out calibration set and may over-fit if that set is small or distribution-shifted. 3) The approach assumes post-click behaviors are causally related to conversion; in adversarial or bot traffic, behaviors may be fabricated, degrading predictions. 4) The method is evaluated on two datasets only — generalizability to platforms with very different conversion delay distributions (e.g., multi-day e-commerce vs. seconds-scale app installs) is unclear. 5) The homoscedastic uncertainty formulation assumes output noise is input-independent, which is a simplification for behavior signals with high user heterogeneity.
interview_questions: Why do post-click user behaviors help alleviate the delayed feedback problem, and what is the key temporal assumption the method relies on?
How does the uncertainty-based adaptive loss function work, and why is it preferable to manually tuned task weights in this setting?
What is the difference between this approach and ESMM/ESM2 in terms of how post-click behaviors are used?
Why is calibration (not just ranking AUC) particularly important for CVR models in bidding systems, and what metric does parameterized scaling specifically optimize?
What failure modes would you expect if post-click behavior data is extremely sparse or delayed for certain user segments?
interview_answers: Post-click behaviors (add-to-cart, favorites, extended dwell) occur within seconds-to-minutes after a click, while actual conversions (purchases) may take hours or days. The method exploits this shorter latency: by the time the model needs to produce a CVR estimate for real-time bidding, behavioral signals are already observable, whereas conversion labels are not yet available. The key assumption is that behaviors are strongly predictive of eventual conversion, so predicting them accurately transfers representational benefit to the CVR head.

The method adopts homoscedastic (task-level) uncertainty weighting from Kendall & Gal (2018): each task loss is divided by twice its learned variance, and a log-variance regularization term is added. This means the model automatically increases the weight of tasks it finds hard (high uncertainty) and reduces weight on tasks it finds easy. This is preferable to manual tuning because the optimal weighting changes as training progresses, and because different datasets have different behavior-to-conversion correlation strengths that are hard to anticipate a priori.

ESMM uses post-click behaviors (specifically purchase) to decompose CVR estimation via the CTR×CVR product and trains on impression-level data to avoid sample selection bias, but it does not explicitly predict intermediate behaviors as targets — they are structural assumptions in the factorization. ESM2 adds more behavior stages. This paper treats each behavior type as a dedicated prediction task head, making behavioral prediction an explicit supervision signal rather than a structural prior, and then adds post-hoc calibration on top, which ESMM/ESM2 do not address.

In second-price or first-price auctions, bids are set proportional to predicted CVR × value. A model with good AUC rank but poor calibration (e.g., systematically under-predicting by 2×) leads to underbidding and lost auctions, or overbidding if over-predicted, directly damaging revenue and ROI. Calibration metrics like Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and log-loss measure this. Parameterized scaling (e.g., a learned temperature or sigmoid reparameterization) adjusts the output probability distribution to minimize log-loss or ECE on a held-out validation set without altering the ranking order.

Sparse behavior data means the auxiliary task heads receive few gradient updates, effectively reducing to training CVR alone — the multi-task benefit vanishes. Worse, if behaviors are not missing at random (e.g., only power users exhibit measurable behaviors), the learned behavior representations become biased toward those users, and the calibration module may over-correct for a subpopulation. The uncertainty weights may also inflate for sparse behavior tasks, causing the model to assign them low weight and ignore them, making the adaptive loss degenerate. One mitigation would be to gate the auxiliary losses based on minimum behavior count thresholds per item/user.
